sentences.text: Again, we are in-- I am in that camp of [NON-ENGLISH] doing my great job, having that great-- having really-- this is really the first break that I've had in any of these camps, because I never had such easy job.
This was great.
In addition to it, I must say one thing.
The camp commandant there was a German SS man.
And he was almost human.
Now, I must say he was a very fair-minded person.
He tried to secure accelerations for us.
He was a decent human being.
He was-- really, he was.
This was about the only kind-- the only kind word I can say about a German SS man.
And he was the man in charge of the entire camp.
He was a decent person for some reason or another.
He was not a very young man.
He must've been-- you know, my concept of age is a little difficult, but I would say he must've been a man in his mid-40's at the time, and of course, young man-- again, that's relative.
But--
Yes, I think it might have.
It might have.
But he was a decent-- I remember him as being-- I do not remember his name.
But had I ever met-- if I would have met him after the war, I would've been the first one to testify on his behalf, that he was decent and human.
He was not-- he never, ever beat anybody.
He never, ever shot anybody.
He tried to keep law and order, so to say, but in a humane way.
What about the age?
I've met-- I've met some-- I've had some-- I've known some people of the same age group, and they were not at all human.
They were animals, so to say.
He might have been just a decent person.
He was a decent person that joined an SS, or the Nazi, or whatever he did join.
But he was human.
And maybe-- well, it could have also been the fact that this is-- now we are already in 19-- almost in 1945.
And it could've been that he had an inkling that the Germans would lose the war.
And whatever he did, he wanted to protect himself.
And he was looking out into the future already, and seeing-- I don't know what this behavior was a year earlier.
He might have been just as much of an animal.
But when I have known him, he was decent.
In any event, now we are beginning to hear stories that the Russians are making advances.
And the war is getting close to being over.
Now, this is now January already.
All of a sudden, we are being loaded up on cattle trains, and we are going to be shipped further into Germany, because the Russians are coming.
Now, as bad as it was in August being shipped from Plaszow to Mauthausen-- remember, we were in closed cattle cars, going back from Mauthausen to Auschwitz in closed cattle cars.
This time, it is winter, and we are being loaded up into open, coal truck-- coal wagons, so to say, the ones that they transport coal.
Have you ever noticed?
Have you ever seen how they transport coal?
Open cars.
Now, it's freezing cold.
It's winter.
It's snowing.
We are sitting in open cars, being transported away from the Russians, further into Austria.
We didn't know where we were going, but we heard rumors that we are going to be transported to Mauthausen.
That we are going back to Mauthausen for some reason or another.
We are going through Poland.
We are going through Czechoslovakia.
Usually, when we traveled through Poland-- now, here we could observe a lot of things because we were in an open car.
We could observe the reaction of the local population.
Any time we would-- while we were going through Poland, you know, we want-- you go from Poland to Austria, you travel part Poland, then part Czechoslovakia, then you come into Austria.
While we were traveling through Poland, we were getting dirty looks.
They knew we were Jews because we had our star of David and all that sort of thing on our-- on our striped uniforms.
And however, when we travelled to Czechoslovakia, we were getting much more sympathetic looks.
As a matter of fact, I remember one incident.
The train stopped in Czechoslovakia, and there were some old ladies that came to the train, and they saw us.
And they started throwing bread at us.
They were throwing bread at us.
Now, I've never experienced anything like this, where the local population was throwing bread.
Now, here they were now.
It was forbidden, of course.
And there were Germans standing there.
And each time one of these women came close and threw bread, with a butt of the rifle, they were hitting-- they were getting hit with the butt of the rifle.
They weren't shooting them, but they were hitting-- they were hitting them with the butt of the rifles.
Now, these people, knowing very well that they were going to be injured, they really risked their lives.
And they were throwing bread at us.
Now, I can-- I can appreciate anything like this.
So at the-- at the-- at the-- the fear was there.
You could see that they were-- they were actually frightened, but they still threw bread at us.
They were sympathetic, and they were Czechs.
And as I said before, our experience with people in Czech, in the Czechoslovakia, was always much more sympathetic towards us than were the Poles.
Now, there were some Poles, from what I understand-- and I heard stories from fellows that I was with-- that Poles did help them.
There were many instances where the Polish Gentile people did help.
Unfortunately, I've never came across it.
But just because I didn't, that doesn't mean that they didn't help.
They did help.
There were cases where they did help them.
All I could see is from my experience, the Czechs helped.
[LAUGHS]
It gave me great sympathy towards the Czech nation as a whole.
I'll never forget these things because, you see, it is one thing to throw a piece of bread if you have the option do it or don't do it.
Now here, you know damn well you will be hit with a rifle, with a butt of a rifle, and you still do it because you want to do it, you know?
So it's-- it is commendable.
It is very commendable.
Now at-- on that transport going to Mauthausen, there is one particular incident which comes to mind.
We were traveling in open trains, which made it relatively possible to escape because, well.

There was a incident in one of the towns-- and I think it might have been already in Austria-- where the train stopped for one reason or another, and one of the young men said he wants to go off to the bushes.
He wants to do what he has to do in the bushes.
And they let him.
And the man disappeared.
He tried to escape.
They had dogs.
And they sent out a detail with a couple SS soldiers with dogs.
Within 15 minutes, they brought him back.
They found him.
They brought him back.
They laid him down on the ground, near the railroad track.
The commandant of the SS told the, the two young men, the two young SS man that found the one of them, he says, execute him.
So I remember that young soldier, that young SS-- I don't know if he was SS.
He might have been a Wehrmacht.
But he took a rifle, and he pointed at him, and he just couldn't-- he just couldn't quite shoot him.
So the SS man, the commandant again says, execute him.
And again, he took the rifle, and he could see that he-- the SS, he cursed him out.
He pushed him aside.
He took out his own pistol, and shot the man in his head, executed him right there.
He said some very unkind words to that SS man, like you don't deserve to be an SS man, or you don't deserve to be in the service of this nation.
And but that the young man somehow-- that young soldier somehow, he just couldn't do it.
That's the way it looked to me because otherwise, he would have-- he would have shot him.
But he just couldn't do it, and he didn't.
Now, we are coming into Mauthausen several days later.
Many people on that wagon died.
In fact, I do remember I was sitting next to a corpse.
He froze.
He was a man maybe in his 40s.
And I think he was from the island of Rhodes, which is Greek, Greek island-- Jew from Rhodes.
And obviously, he was not as conditioned to the cold weather as some of us from Eastern Europe.
We were more conditioned to the cold weather.
And he froze.
He froze.
He died, so.
But nobody really paid that much attention to it.
As you well know, it's every man for himself.
I beg your pardon?
Well, I paid-- I knew he was dead.
And it was tight in space.
Now, I do remember that one young man wanted to really sit on him to have a seat.
And then a lot of our people says, don't you dare, he says.
Find yourself another space.
Do not sit on a dead body.
I mean, it is-- it just goes against us, you know?
And he didn't.
And he found himself another place because he wanted to make himself a little more comfortable.
Sitting-- instead of sitting on the floor, he was going to sit on a-- on a corpse.
I mean, you could imagine how hardened we were because in today, if I would see a corpse, I would be frightened.
Not that I didn't see enough in my day, but I did.

But at that time, it really didn't have-- you got so hardened, it really didn't have that much of an effect on you as it would have today because many years later, your outlook changes, and so on, and so forth.
A hard decision?
Give me an example.
What do you mean by a hard decision?
Would I ever do-- did I ever do something that I didn't want to do?
I never went out to my way to hurt anybody.
I never-- let me put it to you this way.
I was looking out for number one, and I was number one.
But I would never, ever take away a piece of bread from, from-- I would never steal a piece of bread from another inmate because his life depended upon it.
I just couldn't do it.
I would never, ever inform on anybody else because informing on somebody else was equivalent to passing a death sentence on somebody else.
So even if, if there would've been a reward for it-- and I don't know if they would, or if they wouldn't, but I've never, ever considered it.
I just couldn't do it.
I mean, I wasn't brought up this way.
And many, many-- there were many informers, unfortunately.
Even among our own people, there were.
Now, I give you examples where the--- some of the sort of the kapos in camp were decent.
Many more cases, they were not.
They were informers.
They were collaborators, and they were looking out for themselves without this regard to their fellow inmates.
Now, we have arrived in Mauthausen.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mauthausen, again, I never was put to work in Mauthausen because Mauthausen was stone quarries, and that was murder.
And you made one wrong step, and you ended up in a-- like, have you ever been to Grand Canyon?
All right.
Mauthausen is Grand Canyon in Austria.
Now you're-- you make one wrong move, and you're dead.
And there, many, many people have fallen to their death working in the stone quarry.
Now I never got to work in a stone quarry.
In any event, I was in Mauthausen.
Now, we are in Mauthausen.
And it is-- let me see.
It's already '45.
It is now must be January of 1945.
And Mauthausen, I am a very short time, maybe no more than two or three days.
And I get sent out to a subcamp.
I'm being shipped out again to Ebensee.
Now Ebensee is the last camp that we went to-- that I went to-- because I was liberated in Ebensee.
I got to Ebensee.
Ebensee was a real concentration camp, crematoria, the works.
Mauthausen, of course, is known for it.
That was one of the major camps.
But Ebensee, even though it was a subcamp, they had their own extermination center there.
And this was a very, very rough camp.
And I worked in various jobs, wherever I was needed.
Never two days where I did the same job.
It was always something.
You reported in the morning.
You came to the appelplatz, so to say, and there, from there, you were assigned to go wherever you had to do the work.
The lucky part was whereby it was already February of 1945, and we were getting reports that the Allies were coming close.
They were coming close, and we could see that the behavior of the Germans was sort of-- they were beginning to worry about themselves, the guards.
It was quite obvious.
Now, the administration in Ebensee-- and it was not Jewish.
The administration in Ebensee were Germans, German prisoners.
We had German Gentiles.
We had Russian Gentiles.
We had some other nationalities.
We had some even Polish people.
Polish Gentiles were there.
In addition to the Jews, there were Polish gentiles.
But the inner administration was primarily conducted by the Germans.
Now the Germans, the German Gentiles, very brutal, extremely.
Now these were hardened criminals.
They were there not because they were Germans.
They were there because they committed crimes.
They were rapists.
They were thieves.
There were homosexuals, they were-- which at that time was considered a crime in Germany.
They were some political prisoners.
Now, the politicals were kept separate.
They had an insignia, like a red insignia.
And they were, from the Germans, they were the most humane ones, were the political prisoners.
They were communists, actually.
But they, basically, did not have a very high opinion of the other German prisoners.
Now the Germans, through the administration, which was the blockalteste and the captain of the block-- they were all Germans.
They lived in the same thing.
But they, they, they, they were in charge of rations.
They were in charge of everything inside the camp.
These people, I heard them talk.
They were really talking already.
They knew that the Americans were getting close, that the war is coming to an end.
But they were going to line up in the last day with the SS and fight on the side of the Germans against us, in case we ever tried something funny.

If we tried to revolt against the guards, they were not-- they were going to line up with the Germans and helped the Germans, he says, because their hope was-- he says, we are Germans, and we must protect our nation.
Even though they were prisoners, and they were in a concentration camp, they considered themselves Germans, and they were proud Germans.
So no matter what-- no matter what, in the last minute, we could not ever count on them being on our side.
They were going to join with the SS, and they were hoping that the SS were going to give them weapons, and they were hoping that they were going to help the SS exterminate us, the Jews.
So basically, we had an enemy within, and we had an enemy outside.
The outside enemy, of course, was the SS.
Inside enemy was the German administration that was running the camp.
Fortunately, a lot of them were caught and hung by us right after, after-- because when the Germans ran away, the SS, they didn't take them with them.
They left them there, and we did a good job on them.
[LAUGHS] We got-- we caught most of them later on.
But now, we are coming-- we're getting close to May-- we're already in May of 1945.
And one morning, we wake up, and we look out, and there is nobody up on top and in the guard house, empty.
We look around.
We go out.
We dare to go out.
There is no Germans around.
It looks like everybody-- everything gone.
We thought it was some kind of a trick because after all, I mean, they think maybe we will try to escape, and they'll shoot us.
There'll be an easy way out.
So now what happens is we sit, sit tight.
We sort of sit.
We are with them.
Many, many sick people in our-- in our barracks.
We are all young people-- some older ones-- but predominantly young people, like myself.
And we sit there, and we wait.
And about in the afternoon-- it must've been around 3 o'clock, all of a sudden, we see the door opens.
[CRYING]
And we see the American soldier.
Must have been a young man, maybe 20, maybe 22, heavyset with a big helmet.
He comes in.
He looks at us, and he starts crying.
He cries.
One of us-- one of our boys, I don't know how-- but he comes out and starts singing 'Yankee Doodle Dandy.' [CRYING] And we all join in.
We don't know the words.
We don't know the music.
But he starts singing 'Yankee Doodle Dandy.' And we all start singing 'Yankee Doodle Dandy.' And as soon as this one first American soldier comes in, there's a whole battalion of American soldiers.
And we are standing there singing 'Yankee Doodle Dandy' without knowing the words.
And they stand and cry.
They look at us, and they cry.
I mean, battle-hardened soldiers, men that probably were in the Battle of the Bulge, or whatever they were fighting.
They stand.
They look at us, and cry.
And we stand there, cry with them, and sing 'Yankee Doodle Dandy.' That scene is something which I'll never forget.
I mean, [SOBBING] it was like saying, what took you so long?
Where were you?
[CRYING] Now, we were talking to them in our own language.
They didn't-- they, of course, were Americans.
And Americans, very few Americans speak a foreign language.
But I spoke a little English.
Remember my English lessons?
[LAUGHTER] Came in handy.
So I, of course, started speaking to the soldiers.
And of course, they grabbed me right away, and they said, boy, we need you.
[LAUGHTER] We need you because you will be our interpreter for the time being, until we can.
And I, of course, started right away working with them.
And the following day, now this was the first.
There was the-- that was the first day.
Now, the following day there was a warehouse that the Germans abandoned, which, which they kept our bread rations there.
Now here comes a funny story which is comical in a way.
I mean, right now, it's time for a comical story because now the war is over.
By the way, we heard that Hitler committed suicide, which was a great joy to us.
And we heard that the Germans capitulated.
Admiral Doenitz, Doenitz, whatever his name was, he signed the-- they gave up.
So, of course, this was all a great joy.
So all the-- all the inmates decided to raid the warehouse with bread.
So I'm with them.
We come in there.
We open up.
We break the doors.
And we see a warehouse.
We have never seen it in our life.
I mean, floor to ceiling, bread, loaves of breads, lots and lots of bread.
And it was on these shelves with little ladders.
So we all started climbing these ladders and grabbing breads.
Now I am one of them, and I get up to the top, and I take three loaves of bread because after all, I mean, you don't know what's going to be tomorrow.
Somehow, we still had, had in mind says, my God.
Maybe the Germans make a counter offensive.
Maybe they'll come back, and we'll get caught again.
So let's get all the bread we can.
So I grabbed three loaves of breads.
And I stuff it into my things, all over.
And I go down, and as I come down, I see a Russian prisoner.
He looks at me, sees I have three loaves of bread.
And the thing was empty.
Those, those shelves with bread, within five minutes, it was absolutely gone.
It was finished.
Everybody grabbed.
And nobody grabbed one loaf.
Grabbed as much as you can.
So he sees I have three loaves.
He grabs them.
He takes one loaf and says, you don't need three loaves.
And he grabs one loaf, and takes it and runs away.
I go five steps further, another Russian comes running to me.
He said, you have two loaves?
I got to have one loaf.
He grabs the other loaf.
[LAUGHS]
Finally, on my way to my barrack, another man comes-- I don't know who he was.
He wasn't a Russian, I don't think.
He sees I have a bread.
He says, you cannot have a whole bread.
He grabs the bread.
He breaks it in half.
He says, here.
This one is for you.
This one is for me.
So now I have a half a loaf.
I come to the barrack, and I quickly one quarter of it.
And I says, after all, I have to save the other quarter for tomorrow, because we don't know what tomorrow will bring.
So I take that, and before I go to sleep, first of all, my tummy was full already.
I hide that quarter of a loaf of bread under the mattress, that straw mattress that we are sleeping on.
And I was so happy because I'm going to wake up tomorrow morning, and I'll have breakfast.
I'll have bread.
I wake up in the morning, bread is gone.
[LAUGHS] Somebody must have seen me hide that bread.
It was really immaterial.
I'll just say, because it's the-- I'm trying to bring out the mentality.
Now, as soon as the Americans came, we were never, ever hungry because they brought us all the food they could.
There was absolutely no more hunger.
They, they gave us the best possible care they could.
This was a field hospital that really entered this area.
It was the American-- I think it was called 139th Field Hospital of the United States Army.
And they not only brought food, but they brought medicine.
And they set up tents.
They brought their own army doctors, army nurses.
And all the sick people, whoever couldn't walk, was immediately taken, and they were immediately taken care  of.
I mean, they took care of us like we were the American soldiers.
Now, the first thing that I remember when I-- when they took me to the captain-- because I was probably the only partially English-speaking inmate in there-- they asked me, I know you have Russians.
I know you have Poles.
Are there any Americans?
I says, no, there are no.
Are you sure there are no American citizens?
I'm quite sure.
I would've probably come across.
As a matter of fact, there were no Americans because there were instances where American prisoners were taken into concentration camp.
But in this particular case, there was no American there.
But in any event, they-- now what they have done is the next day, they went to town, to the Austrian town.
They brought in a lot of the men and women from the local population, and they brought them in-- the American army did.
And they had to clean and wash our wounds.
They made them work.
And you should have heard these Germans complaining that we are innocent.
We are the victims.
Why are we going-- why do we have to clean these dirty people?
Why do we-- and they were really cleaning the ones, the disabled ones, the ones that couldn't-- that couldn't care for themselves.
So they had to clean them, and they had to wash them.
Of course, afterwards, they let them go.
They went back.
But they, they, they had to do a little work, and they resented it because after all, it was beneath them to clean and care for Jewish inmates.
How did you feel about it?
How what?
Well, it makes me feel that the Austrians, by and large, were no better than the Germans.
They considered themselves that they are victims.
Now to the best of my recollection, they were not the victims.
If they were victims, they were willing, willing victims.
But they were not-- they were not at all-- just because one was Austrian, to the best of my knowledge, he was no better than a German.
In many cases, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they were as bad or worse than Germans.
So--
No.
The German-- not I myself.
But the group of prisoners, whenever we spotted these, we knew exactly who they were because they were our-- oh, we, we grabbed-- a lot of them were hung right on from the tree.
They were executed because these people were brutal.
I mean, they were-- and a lot of them were dragged into the-- they were taken out and sort of lynched.
A lot of them were-- their throats were slashed.
I mean, the anger of the inmates was such against those, those German kapos that were in there that it was indescribable.
Now, that went on for awhile.
And then, of course, the Americans, as soon as they got things a little organized, they stopped it all.
And they presumably arrested them to give them fair trials and all that sort of thing.
But in Ebensee, I myself saw many, many of these German in their administration, the prisoners, the ones that-- the brutal ones, they were all killed.
The soldiers were gone, so we couldn't catch any soldiers.
But as far as the-- we didn't even, even see the soldiers.
They must have escaped, I think, the night before, or whatever.
And had the Germans, the kapos known about it, they would've probably gone along with them, or they would have escaped themselves.
But they never knew about it.
When they woke up in the morning, the surprise was that the Americans came in.
[LAUGHS]
Good.
